attributes.stem:
A 30-year-old man presents to his general practitioner with a four-week history of a scrotal swelling. He first noticed the swelling after a few bad falls on his skateboard. The left side of the scrotum and contents are normal, and on the right side, there is a firm 3 cm diameter non-tender swelling which does not transilluminate. The ultrasound shows a 3 cm hyperechoic, well-circumscribed heterogeneous mass in the lower pole of the right testis. His blood tests return the following results:

  • Beta HCG: 55 IU/L (<5 IU/L)

  • Alpha fetoprotein: 32 KIU/L (<10 KIU/L)

  • LDH: 230 U/L (120-250)

A right orchidectomy is conducted, and the histology of the cells reveals the presence of mesenchyma of the primitive yolk sac.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely cause for this man's testicular swelling?
attributes.explanation:
A firm, well-circumscribed lesion that does not transilluminate and has hyperechoic imaging on an ultrasound suggests that the lesion is most likely to be solid. An urgent ultrasound must always be considered in males with a mass that does not transilluminate or when the testis itself cannot be clearly palpated. Tumour markers can be considered an initial investigation along with the ultrasound, although this is not routine as markers can be falsely low or raised. Any intra-testicular mass must lead to an urgent urological referral for consideration of orchidectomy. If bilateral orchidectomy was commenced, testosterone replacement would be needed.

Generally, the tumour markers to be considered are as follows:

  • Alpha fetoprotein: not raised in seminoma, but raised in nonseminomatous germ cell tumour

  • Beta-HCG: raised in both non-seminoma and seminoma cancers

If beta HCG is positive, nipple tenderness and gynaecomastia are possible symptoms that can occur.

  • LDH: raised in both non-seminoma and seminoma cancers

The elevated levels of beta HCG and alpha fetoprotein in this patient are indicative of a nonseminomatous germ cell tumour. These markers are typically elevated in such tumours, which include embryonal carcinoma, yolk sac tumour, choriocarcinoma, and teratoma. The presence of both markers suggests a mixed germ cell tumour, which is common in nonseminomatous types.

Furthermore, nonseminomatous germ cell tumours are confirmed by the histological finding of mesenchyma of the primitive yolk sac, which is characteristic of a yolk sac tumour, a subtype of nonseminomatous germ cell tumour. This aligns with the elevated tumour markers seen in the blood tests.

Seminoma typically presents with elevated beta HCG but not alpha fetoprotein, and the histology would not show yolk sac elements. Therefore, it is less likely in this scenario.

Germinoma is a term often used interchangeably with seminoma in males, and thus would not fit the histological findings or the tumour marker profile.

Gonadoblastoma is a rare tumour that usually occurs in individuals with gonadal dysgenesis and would not typically present with the elevated tumour markers or histological findings described.

Sertoli cell tumour is a type of sex cord-stromal tumour and does not typically present with elevated beta HCG or alpha fetoprotein, nor would it show yolk sac elements on histology.
learningPoints: Tumour markers can be a starting point for a diagnosis of testicular cancer.
Nongerminomatous germ cell tumours often present with elevated alpha fetoprotein and beta-HCG levels.
Histological findings of mesenchyma of the primitive yolk sac are indicative of a yolk sac tumour, a type of nongerminomatous germ cell tumour.
references: Horwich A, Nicol D, Huddart R. Testicular germ cell tumours. BMJ. 2013 Sep 24;347.
Baird DC, Meyers GJ, Hu JS. Testicular Cancer: Diagnosis and Treatment. Am Fam Physician. 2018; 97:261-68

Kattuoa Ml, Dunton CJ. Yolk Sac Tumors. [Updated 2023 Feb 4]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK563163/

Batool A, Karimi N, Wu XN, Chen SR, Liu YX. Testicular germ cell tumor: a comprehensive review. Cell Mol Life Sci. 2019 May;76(9):1713-1727. doi: 10.1007/s00018-019-03022-7. Epub 2019 Jan 22. PMID: 30671589; PMCID: PMC11105513.

Batool A, Karimi N, Wu XN, Chen SR, Liu YX. Testicular germ cell tumor: a comprehensive review. Cell Mol Life Sci. 2019 May;76(9):1713-1727. doi: 10.1007/s00018-019-03022-7. Epub 2019 Jan 22. PMID: 30671589; PMCID: PMC11105513.

Rajpert-De Meyts E, Aksglaede L, Bandak M, et al. Testicular Cancer: Pathogenesis, Diagnosis and Management with Focus on Endocrine Aspects. [Updated 2023 Mar 29]. In: Feingold KR, Anawalt B, Blackman MR, et al., editors. Endotext [Internet]. South Dartmouth (MA): MDText.com, Inc.; 2000-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK278992/
